ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for stir-fried beehoon with chicken and egg; mapped to the closest parent class 'rice noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (rice noodles, stir-fried) is the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for the chicken-and-egg variant in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried beehoon with chicken and egg served at a Singapore hawker centre typically weighs approximately 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Beehoon with Chicken and Egg — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Stir Fried Beehoon with Chicken and Egg' (cr_id F05060123). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for stir-fried beehoon with chicken and egg. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice vermicelli (GI ~61, medium category). Stir-frying with oil and protein (chicken, egg) and vegetables (chye sim, mushroom) may modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain boiled vermicelli; the estimate of 61 is therefore a conservative upper bound. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (61) × available carbohydrate per serving (300 g × 16.34 g/100g = 49.0 g) / 100 = 29.9, rounded to 30.0. Note: a slightly higher GL of 32.0 is used to account for portion variability.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 122 mg/100 g — well below the 200 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is low at 122 mg/100 g; individuals with hypertension can include this dish in moderate portions provided soy sauce and added salt are kept minimal during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli is a refined carbohydrate with a medium-to-high GI (~61); dietary fibre is low at 1.73 g/100 g, limiting glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre, and consider substituting beehoon with konjac noodles to reduce postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is moderate at 3.85 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat of 0.8 g/100 g; sodium is low at 122 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and sodium profile makes this dish broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should ensure cooking oil is minimised and choose lean chicken breast to keep saturated fat low.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Beehoon with Chicken and Egg is a popular Singaporean hawker dish made from thin rice vermicelli noodles (beehoon) wok-fried with sliced or shredded chicken, beaten egg, chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage), and dried black mushrooms. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, garlic, and a small amount of cooking oil. The noodles are light and slightly chewy, absorbing the savoury umami flavours of the soy sauce and mushrooms, while the egg adds richness and the chye sim provides a mild bitterness and crunch. Per 100 g, the dish provides 130 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 3.85 g fat, and 16.34 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 122 mg and dietary fibre at 1.73 g. The protein from chicken and egg makes this a more nutritionally complete noodle dish compared to plain fried beehoon. Clinically, the dish is low in sodium and saturated fat, making it suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular management in moderate portions. However, rice vermicelli is a refined carbohydrate with a medium GI (~61), and the low fibre content means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate their portion size and pair the dish with additional vegetables. This dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops and is commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, or dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) with a confidence score of 0.95 for all core macros and secondary nutrients. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous food (boiled rice vermicelli, GI ~61) and is documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat of 0.8 g/100 g is estimated from the HPB hypertension suitability note in the input record, which states 'saturated fat is low (0.8g/100g)'; this is consistent with the total fat of 3.85 g/100 g. The input record incorrectly classified the cuisine as 'western' and included 'vegetarian' as a dietary tag despite the dish containing chicken and egg; these errors have been corrected. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and soy sauce. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (boiled rice vermicelli); no direct GI measurement for this specific stir-fried preparation was found in approved sources.
